tool_args.label: ADA Compliance Report December 2022
tool_args.raw_text: Standard Follow-Up Report: Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) Compliance Report - December 2022

payload.title: Standard Follow-Up Report: Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) Compliance Report
payload.description: Annual update on the status of compliance with the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) at MTA New York City Transit, summarizing activities for 2022 including Key Station Plan, bus accessibility and maintenance of accessibility features.

warnings: source.publisher is relation context, not a closed enum or identity key. Publisher has 39 accepted/canonical observations and should link to entity context when useful. Suggested relation: published_by.

payload.description: Transit agency responsible for ADA compliance, bus and subway transportation in New York City.
Metropolitan Transportation Authority, which reached a class action settlement affirming commitment to accessibility in the subway system.
MTA Bus, referenced alongside MTA NYCT in wheelchair ramp usage statistics.
All-volunteer group of 18 community members committed to working with NYCT on a range of accessibility issues, established in February 2019 as successor to the CCC.
Successor to the Transportation Disabled Committee, as a method for the disability community to provide input in the selection of Key stations and a forum on NYCT accessibility initiatives.

payload.title: Acting Senior Vice President Stations Business Unit
payload.description: Prepared the Standard Follow-Up Report on ADA Compliance.

tool_args.label: Mayor's Office for People with Disabilities (MOPD)

payload.entity_name: Mayor's Office for People with Disabilities
payload.description: Receives annual reports on the status of ADA compliance within NYCT.

payload.title: Governor
payload.description: Governor who announced the class action settlement between the MTA and accessibility advocates in June 2022.

NYCT program committing to additional accessible stations. Initially committed to 50 additional accessible stations in 2018, increased to 70 in 2019. Goal: ensure no subway rider is more than two stops away from an accessible station anywhere in the system.

Plan to make 100 subway stations accessible to people with disabilities, originally set by 2020. Specified 91 of 100 stations initially with remaining 9 to be identified. 99 Key stations are now accessible.
The final Key station in the Revised Key Station Plan, currently under construction for accessibility.

Governor Kathy Hochul announced that the Metropolitan Transportation Authority and accessibility advocates reached a class action settlement affirming the MTA's commitment towards accessibility in the subway system. Under the terms, the MTA will add elevators or ramps to create a stair-free path of travel at 95 percent of currently inaccessible subway stations by 2055.

tool_args.label: Public Event on Station Prioritization - February 6, 2019

payload.description: A public event where every geographic area across the system was reviewed and feedback was received on priority stations for accessibility in the next capital program.
The last ACTA (Advisory Committee for Transit Accessibility) Meeting in 2022 was held in July 2022.

payload.claim_text: The bus fleet, which has nearly 6,000 buses, is 100% accessible with wheelchair ramps or lifts, securement devices, public address systems, required priority seating signage and kneeling features.
payload.subject: MTA NYCT and MTA Bus bus fleet

payload.claim_text: Under the terms of the agreement, the MTA will add elevators or ramps to create a stair-free path of travel at 95 percent of the currently inaccessible subway stations by 2055.
payload.subject: MTA

payload.claim_text: In 2005 the Office of ADA Compliance partnered with the Department of Subways to administer the ADA Sensitivity Training Course. All Bus Operators have received ADA training on announcements, priority seating, kneeling features and sensitivity with respect to differences among disabilities.
payload.subject: NYCT Office of ADA Compliance and Department of Subways

payload.claim_text: In 2019, MTA NYCT successfully created and launched an e-learning module that provides an overview on sensitivity training, stressing disability etiquette and knowledge of accessibility features across our systems, in an interactive video format. Going forward, all MTA NYCT employees will complete this e-learning module annually.
payload.subject: MTA NYCT employees

payload.description: Customers who used the wheelchair ramp or lift to access MTA NYCT and MTA Bus from January through December 2022.
Customers who used the wheelchair ramp or lift to access MTA NYCT and MTA Bus in 2021.
Increase in wheelchair ramp or lift usage from 2021 to 2022.

Approximately 61% of the bus fleet installed with DIS as of end of 2022.

payload.raw_value_text: More than 2,978 Help Point units installed
payload.description: More than 2,978 Help Point units installed in all 472 NYCT subway stations.
Deficiencies found and referred for action from January through December 2022.
The bus fleet has nearly 6,000 buses.

ADA-specified platform edge detectable warning strips installed during rehabilitation of Key and Non-Key Stations. NYCT adopted a policy requiring installation during rehabilitation of all Key and Non-Key Stations.

Digital Information Screen (DIS) system providing automated stop announcements in text and audio on buses. All new buses delivered with DIS. As of end of 2022, 3,583 of 5,847 buses installed with DIS (approximately 61%).

payload.treatment_kind: public address and customer information screens
payload.description: PA/CIS Program providing text information accompanied by audio messages. All 472 NYCT subway stations have PA/CIS with more than 1,800 units installed throughout the system.

Help Points units are intercoms which customers can use to reach NYCT personnel in case of emergency or to request information and are equipped with induction loop technology for customers with hearing loss. More than 2,978 Help Point units installed in all 472 NYCT subway stations.
Tactile-Braille signs routinely incorporated in capital construction contracts. All Key Stations will have station identification and route signs that comply with ADA Guidelines.
New York Public Buildings Law and Transportation Law amended (Chapter 6.10, L. 1994) to require MTA New York City Transit to expand its Key Station Plan from 54 to 100 accessible stations by 2020.
The Advisory Committee for Transit Accessibility (ACTA) was established as a successor to the CCC. ACTA is an all-volunteer group of 18 community members.
ACTA held its first meeting.
The ADA Compliance Report is published by MTA New York City Transit.
Fast Forward program committed to by NYCT.

tool_args.label: Revised Key Station Plan implemented by NYCT

payload.description: Revised Key Station Plan implemented by MTA New York City Transit.
The class action settlement involves the Metropolitan Transportation Authority.
Governor Kathy Hochul announced the class action settlement.
ACTA was established as a successor to the NYCT ADA Compliance Coordination Committee (CCC) in February 2019.
ACTA is committed to working with NYCT on a range of accessibility issues.
Annual reports are sent to the Mayor's Office for People with Disabilities (MOPD) on the status of ADA compliance within NYCT.
